senses.definition: a personal investment or stake in a particular outcome, which motivates a person to be committed to achieving success or avoiding failure

examples.x: The company's executives have a lot of skin in the game because a significant portion of their compensation is tied to the company's stock performance.

The shareholders voted against the executives' large bonuses because they felt the executives did not actually have enough skin in the game since the bonuses were guaranteed regardless of the company's performance.

senses.definition: to have an interest in the outcome of a particular situation

examples.x: As a shareholder in the company, I have a dog in the fight and will advocate for decisions that benefit the organization.
She has a dog in the fight when it comes to the debate on healthcare reform since her family relies on accessible and affordable medical services.

senses.definition: to offer a beneficial or valuable contribution or resource in a collaborative setting to achieve a common goal

examples.x: They’re looking for candidates who can bring fresh ideas to the table.

word_list.pronunciation: ɐ pˈiːs slˈaɪs ʃˈɛɹ ʌvðə pˈaɪ

senses.definition: a share of money or benefits that are available to one

examples.x: The company made big profits and the workers want a slice of the pie.
Virtually every academic institution, it seemed, wanted a piece of the pie.

word_list.pronunciation: mˌeɪk hˈeɪ wˌaɪl ðə sˈʌn ʃˈaɪn

senses.definition: to take advantage of a favorable opportunity or circumstance while it is available, rather than waiting or procrastinating

examples.x: I had some free time in class, so I made hay while the sun shone and did the extra assignment.
Kim Kardashian makes hay while the sun shines by promoting three products all at once.

senses.definition: material or information that can be used or processed, especially to generate further discussion, ideas, or productivity

examples.x: The controversy surrounding the new policy provided grist for the mill of public debate.
The criticism she received was grist for the mill, as it motivated her to work even harder.
The personal setbacks he faced became grist for the mill of his resilience and determination.
The unexpected challenges became grist for the mill of their problem-solving skills.
The writer drew inspiration from her life experiences, using them as grist for the mill of her creative process.

senses.definition: to have a significant impact or influence

examples.x: A little bit of kindness can go a long way in brightening someone's day.
A little research goes a long way to help eliminate college debt.
Investing in education can go a long way towards improving a nation's future.

senses.definition: to change a situation in the favor of a person, group, etc.

examples.x: I favor anything that tilts the scales in my direction.
Introducing new fish to the lake tipped the balance and made it very hard for the trout that were already living there.
These great men on the wall had done so much to tip the scales in justice's favor.

word_list.pronunciation: stˈænd ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ ɪn ɡˈʊd stˈɛd

senses.definition: to be advantageous or beneficial to someone or something in the long run

examples.x: His language skills will stand him in good stead when he is traveling.
My years of teaching stood me in good stead.
The progress being made under HLAD will stand the country in good stead when negotiations begin.
These two games here will stand them in good stead for the future.

word_list.pronunciation: hæv ðə wˈɪnd æt wˈʌnz bˈæk

senses.definition: to have favorable conditions or circumstances that support or propel one's progress or success

examples.x: The president has the wind at his back on this issue.
